intro.heading: What are engineering protocols?
intro.lead: Eight rules I follow when designing and building software — written so anyone can understand them.
intro.body: This is not abstract philosophy. Each protocol is a concrete decision filter: how to start a project, choose technology, handle routine work, and keep a product alive for years. If you are not an engineer, read the summary and example on each card — they show how these rules work in real projects.

01-architecture-before-implementation.title: Architecture before implementation
01-architecture-before-implementation.summary: Plan how the system is structured before writing code.
01-architecture-before-implementation.body: Before building, I map how parts connect — data flows, modules, and responsibilities. This prevents costly rework when the product grows or requirements change.
01-architecture-before-implementation.example: For an order system, first define how catalog, cart, payment, and warehouse interact — then implement each part.

02-business-value-before-hype.title: Business value before technology hype
02-business-value-before-hype.summary: Technology serves the product, not the other way around.
02-business-value-before-hype.body: I choose tools based on what the business needs to achieve, not what is trending. A reliable stack that solves the problem beats a flashy one that does not.
02-business-value-before-hype.example: If exporting to Excel saves users two hours a day, that is more valuable than adding a microservices stack nobody asked for.

03-automation-over-repetitive-work.title: Automation over repetitive work
03-automation-over-repetitive-work.summary: If a task repeats, automate it.
03-automation-over-repetitive-work.body: Manual work that happens again and again is a signal to build a script, pipeline, or integration. People should focus on decisions, not copy-paste.
03-automation-over-repetitive-work.example: Deployments, test runs, data imports, and report generation are all candidates for automation.

04-maintainability-over-shortcuts.title: Maintainability over shortcuts
04-maintainability-over-shortcuts.summary: Build so the system can be supported for years.
04-maintainability-over-shortcuts.body: Quick hacks accumulate into technical debt. I prefer solutions that the next developer — or future me — can understand and change without fear.
04-maintainability-over-shortcuts.example: Clear naming, consistent patterns, and documented decisions instead of magic one-liners nobody dares to touch.

05-clean-code-over-clever-code.title: Clear code over clever code
05-clean-code-over-clever-code.body: Code is read far more often than it is written. Simple, explicit logic survives team changes and production incidents better than cryptic shortcuts.
05-clean-code-over-clever-code.example: A straightforward function with a descriptive name beats a one-line puzzle that takes an hour to decode.

06-systems-thinking.title: Systems thinking over isolated features
06-systems-thinking.summary: Every feature lives inside a larger system.
06-systems-thinking.body: A new button is not just UI — it touches data, permissions, performance, and other modules. I evaluate changes by their impact on the whole product.
06-systems-thinking.example: Adding export to PDF means thinking about file size, server load, user permissions, and how it fits existing reports.

07-documentation-is-product.title: Documentation is part of the product
07-documentation-is-product.body: If users or developers cannot understand how the system works, the product is not finished. Documentation reduces support load and speeds up onboarding.
07-documentation-is-product.example: API docs, setup guides, and architecture notes updated in the same release as the feature they describe.

08-scalability-from-beginning.title: Scalability from the beginning
08-scalability-from-beginning.summary: Consider growth at design time, not after things break.
08-scalability-from-beginning.body: You do not need massive infrastructure on day one, but you do need architecture that will not collapse when users or data multiply. Scaling is a design choice, not an emergency patch.
08-scalability-from-beginning.example: Choosing a database schema and caching strategy that handle ten times the load without rewriting the core.

hero.eyebrow: Website and business system development in Tashkent
hero.title: Turnkey websites and systems for business
hero.description: I build corporate websites, catalogs, client portals, Telegram bots, and internal systems. We lock the scope, price, and timeline first — then ship a working product.
hero.note: Official contract · Mobile-ready · Payment in USD or UZS

services.heading: Clear formats and pricing
services.intro: Pick a format that fits. Scope and timeline are fixed before work starts.
services.currencyNote: // Payment in UZS equivalent is available at an agreed rate

teaser.heading: Services and pricing
teaser.body: Consultation, brochure site, corporate site, business system, and support — full list on the home page.

consult.name: Initial consultation
consult.summary: A short call to clarify the task and choose the right format.
consult.details: 30–40 minutes. Goals, approximate scope, timeline, and budget. No commitment.

landing.name: Landing or brochure site
landing.summary: For a company, service, event, or a single business line.
landing.details: Custom design, mobile layout, inquiry form, domain setup, basic SEO, and launch.

corporate.name: Corporate site or catalog
corporate.summary: For companies that need a full site with sections, products, or services.
corporate.details: Structure, design, admin area when needed, multiple languages, analytics, SEO, and launch.

system.name: Business system or automation
system.summary: Client portal, ERP module, CRM features, Telegram bot, integrations, or an internal system.
system.details: Price is set after reviewing roles, processes, modules, integrations, and security needs.

brief.heading: For complex projects
brief.body: If the system scope is still unclear, you can order a technical project review (Architecture Brief). Deliverable: system structure, modules, user roles, stages, risks, and a preliminary budget.
brief.credit: If you continue development with Codev_Tim, the review fee is credited toward the project budget.

support.heading: Support after launch
support.body: Updates, small improvements, uptime checks, help with domain, server, and integrations.
support.note: Exact format depends on the project. Extra work from $40 / hour.

items.scope: Scope and timeline fixed before start
items.payment: Payment in USD or UZS equivalent

discuss.name: Discuss the task
discuss.body: We clarify business goals and the expected result.

estimate.name: Estimate and contract
estimate.body: We lock scope, timeline, price, and terms.

build.name: Design and development
build.body: We build the product against the agreed plan.

review.name: Review
review.body: We test scenarios and address feedback.

launch.name: Launch and support
launch.body: We publish the system and stay available.
